perturbation.original_text: Company representatives are available during Company's normal business hours which are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. UTC +2, Monday through Friday.
perturbation.changed_text: Company representatives are available during Company's normal business hours which are 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. UTC +2, Tuesday through Thursday.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the availability of company representatives to only three days a week (Tuesday through Thursday) and reducing the daily hours of availability could be deemed unreasonable and potentially violate implied obligations of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it hinders the client's ability to receive necessary support or services. This is especially important, as a website needs to be maintained every day.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably in performing their contractual obligations. By significantly reducing the availability of representatives, the modified text could be interpreted as a breach of this covenant, as it may frustrate the client's reasonable expectations under the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly reduces the availability of company representatives, potentially frustrating the client's reasonable expectations and hindering their ability to receive necessary support. This could be interpreted as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties,' and states that a breach of this duty can lead to liability. By reducing availability, the company may be failing to act in good faith, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition because it imposes a time-sensitive obligation that potentially violates an implied duty.

perturbation.original_text: Client shall make a prepayment of $1,900 and pay the remaining $3,100 on completion of the Scope of Work.
perturbation.changed_text: Client shall make a prepayment of $4,900 and pay the remaining $100 on completion of the Scope of Work.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring a $4,900 prepayment on a $5,000 contract shifts a disproportionate amount of the financial risk to the client. This may violate consumer protection laws related to unfair contract terms, specifically regarding excessive deposits.
perturbation.law_citation: Council Directive 93/13/EEC of 5 April 1993 on unfair terms in consumer contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: The Unfair Contract Terms Directive prohibits contract terms that create a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of the consumer. Requiring almost the entire payment upfront places the client at a disadvantage, particularly if the Company fails to complete the work satisfactorily, potentially violating the Directive.

perturbation.justification: The changed text requires a $4,900 prepayment on a $5,000 contract, shifting a disproportionate amount of financial risk to the client. This contradicts the Unfair Contract Terms Directive, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which prohibits contract terms that create a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of the consumer. Requiring almost the entire payment upfront places the client at a disadvantage, especially if the company fails to complete the work satisfactorily. The Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Council Directive 93/13/EEC of 5 April 1993 on unfair terms in consumer contracts, reinforcing the legal basis for considering this prepayment structure unfair and contradictory to consumer protection principles.

perturbation.original_text: Company will use its best efforts to deliver the Client Website in the time frame specified in the Scope of Work. Terms of the project: 12 weeks from February 8, 2018 to May 3, 2018. Client acknowledges and agrees that any due dates set forth in the Scope of Work are subject to delay if Company does not receive the required materials or documentation in a timely manner or in the required format or if approvals are delayed by Client or if the Scope of Work is changed by Client.
perturbation.changed_text: Company may deliver the Client Website at any time. Client acknowledges and agrees that any due dates are completely at the discretion of the company. Company is not held responsible for any delays.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the initial delivery timeline and stating the Company is not responsible for any delays removes any guarantee and puts undue burden on the client. This could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 states that where a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration, it is valid for a reasonable time, but unless otherwise agreed, may be terminated at any time by either party. The modification that removes any time frame completely contradicts the requirement of reasonableness in a commercial contract for services.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a delivery timeline, while the changed text removes the timeline and states the company is not responsible for delays. This contradicts UCC § 2-309(1), which states that 'The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.' By removing the original timeline and stating the company is not responsible for any delays, the changed text makes the delivery time indefinite and potentially unreasonable, thus contradicting the UCC's requirement for a 'reasonable time.'
